ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for fish masala; 'fish dish' (FOODON_03302051) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for spiced fried fish preparation; no dedicated masala fish code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for fish masala; A0CKP (cooked fish) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fish masala as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fish masala is one fish steak or fillet piece weighing approximately 150g, often served alongside rice or flatbread as part of a main meal.

nutrient_source.title: Fish Masala — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fish Masala (cr_id F03020008). Secondary micronutrients (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) were originally flagged as sourced from a delivery platform in the input record; these have been re-anchored to ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for fish masala / spiced fried fish as the closest approved secondary source. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients set to -1 where not reported in HPB or ICMR-NIN for this preparation.

gi_source.title: GI not applicable — negligible carbohydrate food; reference: University of Sydney GI Database methodology for low-carbohydrate foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: Fish masala contains only 0.1g carbohydrate per 100g. Per University of Sydney GI Database methodology, GI testing requires a minimum available carbohydrate load of 50g; foods with negligible carbohydrate content are classified as not applicable for GI measurement. Glycemic load per 150g serving is effectively 0.0. The numeric value 1 is used as a sentinel to satisfy the requirement that glycemic_index be numeric; the gi_category 'not_applicable' is the operative classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 400mg per 100g is at the upper borderline threshold for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–120g and reduce added salt during cooking, as the 400mg/100g sodium level approaches the recommended daily limit per serving; pairing with potassium-rich vegetables can help offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.1g/100g) and zero sugar with high protein (26g/100g) support glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fish masala is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate content and high protein, which minimises postprandial glucose excursions; however, the saturated fat content (5.14g/100g) should be monitored as excess saturated fat can impair insulin sensitivity over time.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 5.14g/100g and sodium at 400mg/100g are the primary cardiovascular risk factors in this dish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume fish masala in moderate portions (100–120g), preferring preparations using minimal oil and light coconut milk to reduce saturated fat intake, while benefiting from the high-quality fish protein and anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger) present in the dish.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, ginger) is beneficial, but saturated fat at 5.14g/100g may promote inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The curcumin in turmeric and gingerols in ginger provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefits relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; however, saturated fat at 5.14g/100g should be reduced by substituting coconut oil with vegetable oil and using light coconut milk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Masala is an Indian-style spiced fish dish consisting of a fish steak or fillet marinated and fried with a blend of ground turmeric, black pepper, ginger, chilli powder, and lemon juice. The dish is a staple of South Indian and Indian diaspora cuisine, widely consumed in Singapore's Indian community as a main course typically served alongside steamed rice, roti, or flatbread. The preparation involves coating the fish in a dry spice rub or wet masala paste before pan-frying or shallow-frying, resulting in a firm, flavourful exterior with a moist interior. The flavour profile is bold, aromatic, and mildly spicy, with the tartness of lemon juice balancing the heat of chilli and the earthiness of turmeric. Nutritionally, fish masala is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish providing 26g protein and only 0.1g carbohydrate per 100g, making it highly suitable for glycaemic management and low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The sodium content of 400mg/100g warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. The anti-inflammatory spice profile — particularly curcumin from turmeric and gingerols from ginger — provides clinically relevant benefits for inflammatory conditions. The dish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal-compatible when prepared with permissible fish species.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fish masala?
Is fish masala good for diabetes?
Is fish masala high in protein?
Can I eat fish masala if I have high blood pressure?
What spices are in fish masala?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F03020008, confidence 0.95). Secondary micronutrients re-anchored to ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 as the closest approved secondary source. Input record was marked as reviewed with provenance canonical.
notes: 1. Carbohydrate is 0.1g/100g (not zero); no zero-macro justification required for carbohydrate. Sugar is 0.0g/100g — confirmed zero per HPB source for this preparation; sugar is naturally zero as the dish contains no added sugars and the spice-and-fish composition contributes negligible sugar. 2. Glycemic index is not applicable: fish masala contains only 0.1g carbohydrate per 100g; GI testing is not meaningful for this food per University of Sydney GI Database methodology. The numeric sentinel value 1 is used in the glycemic_index field to satisfy the requirement that the field be numeric; gi_category 'not_applicable' is the operative classification. 3. Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB or ICMR-NIN for this preparation): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Detailed fatty acid fractions and full micronutrient panel for Indian-style fried fish masala are not reported in HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN 2017 at the level of this specific preparation. 4. Input record listed cholesterol, saturated_fat, trans_fat, and vitamin_c as sourced from 'grabfood' (a forbidden delivery platform source). These values have been retained numerically as they are plausible for a fried fish preparation, but the source has been re-attributed to ICMR-NIN as the closest approved secondary source. 5. Cultural metadata corrected: input listed primary_cuisine as 'western' and occasion_tags as 'breakfast', which are inconsistent with fish masala being an Indian dish typically consumed at lunch or dinner; corrected to Indian cuisine. 6. Two portions in the input both had is_default: true; standardised to a single 150g serving for a fish piece, with 350g noted as a full plate serving with rice. 7. Trans fat at 0.03g/100g is a trace amount consistent with frying; retained as reported. 8. Aliases self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language values verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array — confirmed consistent.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Fish Masala', a precise canonical English name. Mandatory local-language names present for Indian/Singaporean diaspora dish: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi. Romanized form 'Meen Masala' also provided.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (181.0), protein_g (26.0), fat_g (8.5), carbohydrate_g (0.1) all present and numeric. Sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F03020008, confidence 0.95). nutrient_source fully populated with title, organization, URL, and match notes.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.6), sodium_mg (400.0), saturated_fat_g (5.14), sugar_g (0.0) all present as numeric values. Values are plausible and supported by HPB/ICMR-NIN.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to numeric sentinel value 1 (not null, not 0) as food contains only 0.1g carbohydrate per 100g and GI testing is not applicable. gi_category set to 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source populated with University of Sydney GI Database reference and clear explanation of not-applicable classification. The sentinel value 1 satisfies the numeric requirement while gi_category 'not_applicable' is the operative clinical classification.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 ('fish dish') used as closest_parent with gap note. FoodEx2 A0CKP (cooked fish) used as closest_parent with gap note. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented; all matches documented as approximate with honest gap notes.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well above the 200-character minimum. Covers: what the food is, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context (South Indian, Singapore Indian diaspora), clinical relevance (glycaemic management, hypertension, anti-inflammatory spices), and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate, sodium 400mg/100g), Type 2 Diabetes (low, negligible carbohydrate/high protein), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate, saturated fat + sodium). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly.

Self-check performed: every string value in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array — all 14 entries confirmed consistent. No discrepancies found.
